refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion tied to duty cycle and site conditions, not a functional requirement of the system itself. This separation allows DPs to address enablement (e.g., responsive controls, efficient hydraulics, predictable motion) independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and representative site conditions, the NGHE solution's subsystems (propulsion, actuation, control response, load handling) do not introduce artificial delays or constraints that prevent the cycle from completing within CT_target. Measure end-to-end cycle time in controlled trials; confirm that any shortfall vs. CT_target is attributable to operator technique, site conditions, or load variability—not to system design limitations.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall execute the load phase (bucket fill, material consolidation) without introducing artificial time delays.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall execute the travel phase (loaded and empty) with responsive motion control and predictable acceleration/deceleration.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall execute the dump phase (bucket positioning, material release, bucket retraction) without introducing artificial time delays.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall provide operator/tele-operator with sufficient situational awareness and control responsiveness to execute the cycle efficiently.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose cycle into discrete, sequenced phases (load, travel, dump) with phase-gating logic that enforces strict ordering and allows independent optimization of each phase duration.

DP2: Implement a centralized cycle-time controller that monitors elapsed time against phase budgets and dynamically adjusts operational parameters (speed, acceleration, dwell) to meet p95 target without exceeding it.

DP3: Design modular subsystem interfaces (propulsion, hydraulics, control) such that each subsystem exposes cycle-phase-relevant performance metrics (e.g., load-phase duration, travel speed capability) enabling predictable composition.

DP4: Establish a duty-cycle characterization model (payload mass distribution, terrain profile, ambient conditions) and use it to pre-compute phase-time budgets offline; execute cycle with closed-loop feedback to detect deviations and trigger corrective actions.

DP5: Allocate cycle-time margin (buffer) proportional to variability sources (soil resistance, operator response, mechanical wear) and reserve it for contingency; design control logic to consume margin only when necessary to meet p95 constraint.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2 + DP4 + DP5 (integrated approach): Implement a centralized, duty-cycle-aware cycle-time controller that (a) pre-computes phase budgets from characterized duty-cycle model, (b) monitors real-time phase execution against budgets with closed-loop feedback, (c) manages allocated margin to ensure p95 ≤ CT_target under site conditions and operational variability.

potential_sources: DP2 (controller) is tightly coupled to subsystem performance metrics (propulsion speed, hydraulic response time); requires well-defined, stable interfaces from propulsion and hydraulics DPs.

DP4 (duty-cycle model) depends on accurate characterization of site conditions and payload distribution; if model is incomplete or site conditions change, feedback loop may not converge to p95 target.

DP5 (margin allocation) introduces coupling between cycle-time control and reliability/wear models; margin consumption logic must be coordinated with maintenance scheduling to avoid systematic margin depletion.
Feedback loop closure (DP2) requires low-latency sensor data and actuator response; introduces temporal coupling between control logic and hardware subsystems.
coupling_check.notes: The integrated DP is logically sound and avoids monolithic design; however, it creates information dependencies (subsystem metrics, duty-cycle model, margin state) that must be managed through clear contracts. Decoupling is achieved by treating subsystems as black boxes with published performance envelopes; coupling is localized to the controller and model layers.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Subsystem performance (propulsion, hydraulics, dump mechanism) is repeatable and measurable within acceptable tolerance; if subsystems exhibit high variability or degradation, feedback loop may struggle to maintain p95 target.

ASSUMPTION: Duty-cycle characterization (payload, terrain, ambient) is accurate and representative of actual site conditions; if site conditions deviate significantly (e.g., unexpected soil type, extreme temperature), pre-computed budgets may be invalid.
ASSUMPTION: Operator behavior (e.g., load-phase dwell time, travel speed selection) is consistent and predictable; high operator variability may consume margin faster than anticipated.
RISK: Margin depletion over time due to mechanical wear, hydraulic degradation, or systematic operator behavior drift; requires monitoring and periodic re-characterization.
RISK: Feedback loop instability if control gains are poorly tuned or if sensor noise is high; may cause oscillation around target or overshoot.
RISK: Site conditions (temperature, humidity, soil properties) change during operation; model may become stale, requiring adaptive re-tuning or manual intervention.
RISK: Acceptance criteria (p95 ≤ CT_target) is statistical; requires sufficient sample size and stable conditions to validate; early-stage testing may not reveal tail-behavior issues.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly address variability and margin management: 'The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target (p95 ≤ CT_target) under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, with explicit allocation of cycle-time margin to accommodate subsystem variability, operator behavior, and environmental factors. The solution shall include a duty-cycle characterization process and a feedback mechanism to detect and correct deviations from target performance.' Additionally, decompose DP2 into two sub-DPs: (a) DP2a: Cycle-phase sequencing and budget allocation logic (static, pre-computed), and (b) DP2b: Real-time feedback control and margin management logic (dynamic, adaptive). This separation clarifies the boundary between offline planning and online execution, reducing coupling and improving testability.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized duty-cycle-aware cycle-time controller with phase budget pre-computation, real-time monitoring, closed-loop feedback, and margin management for p95 cycle-time compliance under site conditions and operational variability.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingest real-time phase execution metrics, duty-cycle observations, and operational variability signals (e.g., load, environmental conditions) to feed closed-loop feedback and margin adjustment.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Emit phase budget allocations, cycle-time targets, and margin-aware scheduling directives to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signal when p95 cycle-time approaches or exceeds CT_target, triggering safety-aware operational adjustments or alerts.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. NGHE Solution is the logical home for centralized, site-aware optimization logic. Pre-computation of phase budgets, closed-loop feedback control, and margin management are all algorithmic/control-plane functions native to a solution-level orchestrator. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion (in) and tasking/safety directives (out) cross boundaries. No algorithmic logic leaks to Vehicle or Teleoperation.

Poor containment. Vehicle is a physical/execution platform, not a control/optimization layer. Placing centralized cycle-time control here would scatter logic across multiple vehicle instances, break site-level coordination, and require Vehicle to understand duty-cycle models and margin policies—violating separation of concerns. High diffusion: phase budget logic, feedback loops, and margin management would need to diffuse back to NGHE Solution and Teleoperation.

Poor containment. Teleoperation is a remote command/monitoring interface, not a control algorithm host. Placing cycle-time controller here would couple remote operations to site-specific optimization, break modularity, and create latency/reliability issues for real-time feedback. High diffusion: pre-computation, feedback loops, and margin logic would diffuse to NGHE Solution and Vehicle.

Weak containment. Safety Services is a reactive alert/enforcement layer, not a proactive optimization controller. While p95 compliance has safety implications, the cycle-time controller's core logic (phase budgeting, feedback, margin management) is not inherently a safety function—it is a performance optimization function with safety constraints. High diffusion: algorithmic control logic would diffuse to NGHE Solution; Safety Services would only consume SafetyAlert outputs.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The integrated DP (pre-computation + real-time monitoring + margin management) is fundamentally a centralized, site-aware optimization algorithm. NGHE Solution is the natural home because it (a) operates at the solution/orchestration level where site conditions and duty-cycle models are known, (b) can coordinate phase budgets across multiple Vehicle and Teleoperation instances, (c) owns the feedback loop and margin policy, and (d) can emit tasking and safety directives to downstream subsystems without algorithmic diffusion. Containment is strong (0.92) because all three DP components (pre-computation, monitoring, margin management) are cohesive control-plane functions that belong in a single, centralized module.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution lacks real-time telemetry ingestion capability, closed-loop feedback latency may degrade p95 compliance. Mitigation: Ensure Telemetry interface is low-latency and high-fidelity.

Risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems do not respect Tasking directives (phase budgets, cycle-time targets), margin management will fail. Mitigation: Define clear contract and enforcement in Tasking interface; add observability to detect non-compliance.

Risk: If duty-cycle model is inaccurate or site conditions change rapidly, pre-computed phase budgets may become stale. Mitigation: Implement adaptive re-computation triggered by telemetry anomalies or periodic refresh cycles.

Risk: If SafetyAlert is not acted upon by Vehicle or Teleoperation, p95 compliance may be violated without corrective action. Mitigation: Define escalation policy and ensure SafetyAlert has clear semantics (e.g., reduce load, extend cycle time, halt operations).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly measurable via telemetry ingestion and statistical analysis. NGHE Solution's centralized architecture enables end-to-end observability: phase execution metrics flow in via Telemetry interface, p95 is computed, and SafetyAlert is triggered if threshold is approached. Verification can be automated via continuous telemetry monitoring and periodic compliance reports.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. FR allocation to IM (NGHE Solution) is unambiguous: the centralized duty-cycle-aware controller owns the entire cycle-time optimization logic. Downstream subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation) receive tasking directives and are responsible for execution fidelity. Worksite safety plan integration is explicit via SafetyAlert interface and margin management policy.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP is well-formed and directly addresses FR. The integrated approach (pre-computation + real-time monitoring + margin management) is logically sound: (1) pre-computed phase budgets establish baseline expectations from duty-cycle model, (2) real-time monitoring detects deviations and operational variability, (3) closed-loop feedback and margin management adapt to site conditions to ensure p95 compliance. DP is sufficiently detailed to guide IM design and is testable against acceptance criterion.

diffusion_risks: TELEMETRY LATENCY: If real-time phase execution metrics are delayed or lossy, closed-loop feedback will degrade and margin adjustments may be reactive rather than proactive. Mitigation: Define maximum acceptable latency (e.g., <100ms) and fidelity requirements in Telemetry interface SLA.

TASKING COMPLIANCE: If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems ignore or partially implement phase budget allocations and cycle-time targets, margin management will fail to achieve p95 compliance. Mitigation: Add observability to detect non-compliance; define escalation policy (e.g., reduce load, extend cycle time, halt) in Tasking interface contract.

DUTY-CYCLE MODEL STALENESS: If pre-computed phase budgets are not refreshed when site conditions or operational patterns change significantly, p95 compliance may degrade. Mitigation: Implement adaptive re-computation triggered by telemetry anomalies (e.g., phase execution variance exceeds threshold) or periodic refresh cycles (e.g., hourly).

SAFETY ALERT ACTIONABILITY: If SafetyAlert signals are not acted upon by downstream subsystems, p95 violations will persist without corrective action. Mitigation: Define clear semantics for SafetyAlert (e.g., 'reduce load by X%', 'extend cycle time by Y%', 'halt operations'), assign ownership for response, and monitor compliance.

MULTI-VEHICLE COORDINATION: If multiple Vehicle instances operate concurrently, NGHE Solution must coordinate phase budgets and margin allocation across instances to avoid resource contention or cascading delays. Mitigation: Extend Tasking interface to include vehicle-specific budgets and priority policies; add observability for cross-vehicle phase interactions.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT (0.92). NGHE Solution is the natural and correct home for the integrated DP. All three DP components (pre-computation, real-time monitoring, margin management) are cohesive control-plane functions that require centralized, site-aware orchestration. No algorithmic logic leaks to Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. Interfaces are clean: Telemetry (in) provides observability, Tasking (out) provides directives, SafetyAlert (out) provides safety-aware escalation. Vehicle and Teleoperation remain execution platforms; Safety Services remains a reactive alert layer. Minimal diffusion (0.08) reflects only necessary boundary crossings for data flow and control signaling.

recommended_revisions: TELEMETRY INTERFACE SLA: Specify maximum acceptable latency, fidelity (e.g., phase execution timestamp precision, duty-cycle observation granularity), and error handling for real-time feedback loop. Example: 'Phase execution metrics must be ingested within 100ms of event occurrence with ≥99% delivery reliability.'

TASKING INTERFACE CONTRACT: Define phase budget semantics (e.g., 'load phase must complete within 120s ±10s margin'), cycle-time target format, and margin-aware scheduling directives. Include enforcement mechanism (e.g., Vehicle must acknowledge receipt and confirm execution plan) and non-compliance escalation policy.

SAFETY ALERT SEMANTICS: Specify SafetyAlert trigger conditions (e.g., 'p95 approaches CT_target within 5%'), alert payload (e.g., recommended corrective action: reduce load, extend cycle time, halt), and expected response time from Vehicle/Teleoperation. Example: 'SafetyAlert: p95 cycle time = 95% of CT_target. Recommended action: reduce load by 10% or extend cycle time by 5%. Response required within 30s.'

ADAPTIVE RE-COMPUTATION POLICY: Define triggers for refreshing pre-computed phase budgets (e.g., telemetry variance exceeds threshold, periodic refresh every 1 hour, manual trigger on site condition change). Include rollback mechanism if new budgets degrade p95 compliance.

MULTI-VEHICLE COORDINATION: If multiple Vehicle instances operate concurrently, extend NGHE Solution to allocate phase budgets and margin per vehicle instance, coordinate resource contention, and aggregate p95 compliance across fleet. Add observability for cross-vehicle phase interactions.

OBSERVABILITY & MONITORING: Implement continuous telemetry-based monitoring of (a) phase execution vs. budget, (b) p95 trend over time, (c) margin utilization, (d) Tasking directive compliance, (e) SafetyAlert frequency and response latency. Expose via dashboards and automated alerts for operational teams.

DUTY-CYCLE MODEL VALIDATION: Document assumptions in duty-cycle model (e.g., load distribution, environmental conditions, vehicle state). Define validation protocol to confirm model accuracy at site commissioning and periodic re-validation (e.g., quarterly). Include fallback to conservative budgets if model accuracy degrades.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR-DP-IM coherence and high verification feasibility, but recommended_revisions expose critical sub-requirements that must be decomposed to ensure safety-critical compliance. The DP is logically sound, but its realization depends on well-defined interface contracts (Telemetry SLA, Tasking semantics, SafetyAlert actionability) and adaptive governance (re-computation policy, multi-vehicle coordination, observability). These are not implementation details—they are functional requirements in their own right that must be explicitly derived, allocated, and verified. High safety criticality demands that interface contracts, margin management policies, and alert response mechanisms be treated as first-class FRs with clear acceptance criteria, not deferred to design-time decisions.
next_level_sub_frs: FR 1.1: Ensure real-time phase execution telemetry is ingested with latency ≤100ms and ≥99% delivery reliability to enable closed-loop feedback for margin management.
FR 1.2: Enforce Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems' compliance with phase budget allocations and cycle-time targets via tasking directives with acknowledgment and execution confirmation.

FR 1.3: Trigger and communicate SafetyAlert signals with clear, actionable semantics (e.g., recommended corrective actions: reduce load, extend cycle time, halt) and enforce response within 30s to prevent p95 violations.

FR 1.4: Refresh pre-computed phase budgets adaptively when site conditions or operational patterns change (telemetry variance exceeds threshold or periodic refresh ≤1 hour) to maintain p95 compliance accuracy.
FR 1.5: Coordinate phase budgets and margin allocation across multiple concurrent Vehicle instances to avoid resource contention and ensure fleet-wide p95 compliance.

FR 1.6: Provide continuous observability of phase execution vs. budget, p95 trend, margin utilization, Tasking compliance, and SafetyAlert response latency via dashboards and automated alerts for operational teams.

revise.why: DP must be expanded to explicitly include interface contract specifications (Telemetry SLA, Tasking semantics, SafetyAlert payload and response expectations) and governance policies (adaptive re-computation triggers, multi-vehicle coordination logic, observability requirements). Current DP is correct in concept but incomplete in specification detail necessary to guide IM design and verification. Revisions should be incorporated as DP sub-components or as explicit design constraints that flow into sub-FR decomposition.

assumptions: Telemetry infrastructure can deliver phase execution metrics with <100ms latency and ≥99% reliability under site conditions.
Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems can reliably receive, interpret, and execute phase budget directives and cycle-time targets.
SafetyAlert signals are acted upon by downstream subsystems within 30s of issuance.
Duty-cycle model assumptions (load distribution, environmental conditions, vehicle state) remain valid across operational shifts; re-validation is feasible at commissioning and periodic intervals.
Multi-vehicle coordination does not introduce deadlock or cascading delay risks; resource contention is manageable via priority policies.
Operational teams have capacity and tools to monitor continuous observability streams and respond to alerts.
open_risks: TELEMETRY LATENCY DEGRADATION: If real-time metrics are delayed >100ms or delivery reliability drops <99%, closed-loop feedback becomes reactive; margin management may fail to prevent p95 violations. Mitigation: Define and validate Telemetry SLA at commissioning; implement fallback to conservative budgets if SLA is breached.

TASKING NON-COMPLIANCE: If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems ignore phase budgets or cycle-time targets, margin management logic is ineffective. Mitigation: Add compliance observability; define escalation policy (reduce load, extend cycle time, halt) and enforce via Tasking interface contract.

SAFETY ALERT INACTION: If SafetyAlert signals are not acted upon, p95 violations persist. Mitigation: Define clear alert semantics, assign ownership for response, monitor response latency, and escalate if response is delayed >30s.

DUTY-CYCLE MODEL STALENESS: If pre-computed budgets are not refreshed when site conditions change, p95 compliance degrades. Mitigation: Implement adaptive re-computation triggered by telemetry anomalies or periodic refresh; include rollback if new budgets degrade compliance.

MULTI-VEHICLE RESOURCE CONTENTION: Concurrent Vehicle instances may compete for shared resources, causing cascading delays. Mitigation: Extend NGHE Solution to allocate per-vehicle budgets, coordinate via priority policies, and aggregate p95 compliance across fleet.

OBSERVABILITY GAPS: If phase execution, margin utilization, and alert response metrics are not continuously monitored, operational teams cannot detect degradation or respond proactively. Mitigation: Implement comprehensive telemetry-based monitoring and expose via dashboards.
evidence_needed: Telemetry infrastructure specification: latency profile, delivery reliability, error handling, and SLA validation protocol.
Tasking interface contract: phase budget semantics, cycle-time target format, acknowledgment/confirmation mechanism, and non-compliance escalation policy.
SafetyAlert specification: trigger conditions, payload format, recommended corrective actions, and expected response time from Vehicle/Teleoperation.
Adaptive re-computation policy: triggers (telemetry variance threshold, periodic refresh interval), rollback mechanism, and validation of new budgets against p95 compliance.
Multi-vehicle coordination design: per-vehicle budget allocation, resource contention resolution, priority policies, and fleet-wide p95 aggregation logic.
Observability and monitoring design: metrics to be tracked, dashboard design, automated alert thresholds, and operational team response procedures.
Duty-cycle model validation protocol: assumptions documentation, commissioning validation, periodic re-validation schedule, and fallback to conservative budgets if accuracy degrades.

Integration test plan: end-to-end verification of Telemetry ingestion, Tasking directive execution, SafetyAlert triggering and response, and p95 compliance under simulated site conditions and failure scenarios.
